sonnet_review.reason: The phrase appears in the headline and lede in the author’s voice and can carry a politically charged tone. While it may be descriptive, it is reasonable to view it as loaded language.
“Expeditiously” is common bureaucratic/legal phrasing and not clearly value-laden in this context. The article uses it descriptively without overt evaluative spin.

sonnet_review.biasedText: Speeding up the court process for immigration matters will, in turn, lead to faster decisions and more deportations
sonnet_review.reason: The sentence is explicitly attributed to Andrew Arthur (“according to former immigration judge Andrew Arthur”), mitigating claims of authorial slant. The piece also includes other perspectives elsewhere.

sonnet_review.biasedText: according to former immigration judge Andrew Arthur... resident fellow in law and policy for the Center for Immigration Studies, an organization that advocates lower levels of immigration
sonnet_review.reason: While Arthur is quoted extensively, the article also cites/quotes other sources (e.g., Charles Kuck, Austin Kocher/TRAC, Axios). Claiming undue deference to one side is not well supported by the balance of sources presented.

sonnet_review.biasedText: The article quotes only Andrew Arthur (from a restrictionist organization) and Charles Kuck (an immigration lawyer) as expert voices
sonnet_review.reason: The article also includes Austin Kocher’s analysis and references TRAC data and Axios reporting. The claim that only Arthur and Kuck are quoted as expert voices is factually incorrect.

gpt_review.reason: The term is used in the author’s narrative and can be perceived as loaded. A more neutral alternative could have been used, so the detection is defensible.

gpt_review.biasedText: moved to simplify the appeals process, reducing the options illegal immigrants have to appeal their deportation orders and shortening their time in the system
gpt_review.reason: The sentence presents both ‘simplify’ and its effects; describing reduced appeal options is factual and not clearly framed as evaluative spin. It mixes neutral and descriptive elements without obvious bias.
This metaphor is a subjective, editorializing characterization of the court’s workload and can reasonably be viewed as biased word choice.
